slug: bulletin-1-The Southern Nigeria Sustainability and Infrastructure Resilience Initiative (SNSIRI)
title: bulletin 1The Southern Nigeria Sustainability and Infrastructure Resilience Initiative (SNSIRI)
summary: Field observation of environmental and infrastructure conditions at Igbo-Etche Central Market and the Eneka–Igbo Etche Link Road corridor revealed severe drainage gaps, waste accumulation, and flood exposure during the rainy season.
shortSummary: Drainage deficit, solid waste accumulation, and unfinished road drainage are exposing the community to flood and erosion risk.
location: Igbo-Etche, Etche LGA, Rivers State, Nigeria

sitesVisited.name: Igbo-Etche Central Market
sitesVisited.type: Local market square / community trading centre

sitesVisited.name: Eneka–Igbo Etche Link Road
sitesVisited.type: Access road under construction / transit corridor

geography.geomorphology: Niger Delta coastal plain — low-lying alluvial terrain
geography.climate: Tropical humid / equatorial — active rainy season June–October
geography.riskNote: Flood-prone classification due to extremely low mean sea level altitude.

keyFindings: The market square and surrounding stall access paths have no formal drainage, causing pooled water after rainfall.
Solid waste is routinely disposed of on open ground or near the community stream, increasing siltation and public health risks.
The active road corridor remains without completed side drains in key stretches, increasing erosion and waterlogging exposure.
Streamside drainage paths are blocked by sediment and debris, threatening food production and water quality.

images.caption: Igbo-Etche Central Market: waste accumulated by the roadside; no collection point present.
Igbo-Etche Streamside / Roadside: flooded roadside and waterlogged access path to farmland.

immediateActions: Clear accumulated solid waste from the market square and streamside area immediately.
Enforce a halt on open waste burning within the market zone.
shortTermActions: Complete side drains along the Eneka–Igbo Etche Link Road within the active rainy season window.
Establish a designated waste collection point for the central market.
mediumTermActions: Design and install market drainage infrastructure to manage runoff.
Launch community sensitisation on waste, flooding, and water quality.
ongoingActions: Repeat field assessments after rainfall to monitor changes.
Build a community environmental stewardship group.
Continue GPS-georeferenced monitoring for advocacy and partner reporting.

slug: bulletin-2-The Southern Nigeria Sustainability and Infrastructure Resilience Initiative (SNSIRI)
title: bulletin 2The Southern Nigeria Sustainability and Infrastructure Resilience Initiative (SNSIRI)
summary: Field observation along the East-West Road corridor (Rumuodara) and Oil Mill Market Junction revealed two distinct failure modes: a residential street completely submerged under floodwater with no drainage infrastructure, and a market junction drain physically present but functionally sealed under accumulated market waste.
shortSummary: Absent drainage at Rumuodara and waste-blocked drainage at Oil Mill Market expose communities to flood risk and waterborne disease contamination.
location: East-West Road (Rumuodara) & Oil Mill Market Junction, Port Harcourt, Rivers State, Nigeria

sitesVisited.name: East-West Road, Rumuodara
sitesVisited.type: Residential access road — fully submerged under floodwater

sitesVisited.name: Oil Mill Market Junction
sitesVisited.type: Market junction — drainage channel buried under refuse

geography.geomorphology: Niger Delta coastal plain — urban low-lying terrain
geography.climate: Tropical humid / equatorial — active rainy season June–October
geography.vegetation: Urban developed area with mixed residential and market zones
geography.riskNote: Flood-prone classification; waterborne disease risk from floodwater proximity to elevated water storage tanks.

keyFindings: East-West Road residential street (Rumuodara) has no kerb-side drain, open gutter, or constructed channel — full carriageway submerged under standing water after rainfall.
Floodwater reaches compound gate thresholds on both sides of the street, with proximity to elevated water storage tank raising contamination concerns.
Oil Mill Market Junction drain exists but is almost entirely buried under mixed market and household refuse — functionally sealed with no water flow.
Decomposing organic matter at Oil Mill Market produces dark leachate staining, putting residents and traders in daily contact with waste and health hazards.
Tricycle still moving through Rumuodara floodwater confirms this is routine, not a one-off event — community has adjusted routines around recurring submersion.

images.caption: East-West Road, Rumuodara: fully submerged residential access road, kerb to kerb, with no drainage channel anywhere in sight.
Oil Mill Market junction: drainage channel buried under uncollected market waste — not broken, not missing, just sealed until it cannot carry a drop of water.

immediateActions: Report Oil Mill Market drainage blockage to Rivers State Waste Management Agency (RIWAMA) — cheapest, fastest intervention identified across both weeks.
Advise nearby households at OBS-004 (Rumuodara) to inspect elevated water tanks for contamination once floodwaters recede.
shortTermActions: Flag missing kerb drainage on Rumuodara access street to Obio/Akpor LGA.
Unblock Oil Mill Market drainage channel — costs a fraction of what building a new one would.
mediumTermActions: Commission a basic drainage capacity check for the Rumuodara residential corridor.
Talk to Oil Mill Market traders' association about a waste collection schedule that keeps the drain clear once reopened.
Add both sites to recurring SN-ESIRI watch list to monitor whether anything changes.
ongoingActions: Walk further along East-West Road corridor in bulletin 3 — Rumuodara flood suggests nearby streets likely flood similarly.
Pull all five GPS points (bulletin 1 + bulletin 2) into one shareable Google My Maps link before next AfWASA and UNICEF submissions.
Continue GPS-georeferenced monitoring for advocacy and partner reporting.
